content:
10 min read
Just now

--

The next major threat to cybersecurity is an autonomous AI adversary. This is how we detect and defeat them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’ve spent my career in cybersecurity focused on on the adversary: more specifically learning about, respecting, and defeating the adversary.
I began my career building encryption to protect systems like the P-8A Poseidon. Back then it was easy for me to learn about my adversary because they were a lot like me. They were a technologist working within or for a foreign country’s military, defense, and/or intelligence community.
It was easy for me to respect them because, regardless of our political or ideological perspectives, we shared a similar incentive structure a skill set. And thus designing and building technology to defeat them was relatively straightforward. I was defeating another version myself, with a different capability set and funding.
But as the years went on, the adversary began to change. New types of adversaries like well-resourced political extremists and organized crime cartels became part of the threat landscape my teams and I were trying to defend against.
This meant that it became harder to learn about my adversary. We no longer were alike, they had wildly different incentive structures and capabilities to mine, and thus defending against them became more complicated.
But these challenges highlight something I love about information security: you never stop learning. Learning about and respecting these adversaries meant immersing myself in worlds I only got to see in movies and on TV.
And in learning to (and ultimately defeating) those adversaries, I got an opportunity to be part of some wild things. 25 year old me may have not gotten as much sleep as he should have. But I don’t know how many other 25 year olds go to work with the FBI on taking down an espionage ring.
Sixteen years on, the adversary has changed again. And this time it’s much harder to learn, respect, and likely defeat them.
Because this time the adversary is no longer human.

The Unsurprising Rise of the Agentic Adversary
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A slide from an investment thesis I wrote in 2016. Automating and simplifying attack tooling to enable adversaries has been a multi-generational trend in security. It also has enabled the creation of a new modern adversary: agentic intelligence trained on decades of this code and writing.On July 21st, OpenAI disclosed that autonomous agents powered by its models had accidentally compromised the infrastructure of LLM repository host HuggingFace.
This accidental compromise stemmed from several cybersecurity agents attempting to complete challenges on ExploitGym. The agents broke out of their containment, and reasoned that to optimally complete the challenge in their eval, they needed to compromise HuggingFace. So they did:
After gaining Internet access, the models inferred that Hugging Face potentially hosted models, datasets and solutions for ExploitGym. Knowing this, the model searched for and successfully found ways to gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation. In one example, the model chained together multiple attack vectors, including using stolen credentials and zero-day vulnerabilities to find a remote code execution path on the Hugging Face servers. OpenAI’s security team discovered this anomalous activity internally.
This is a shocking turn of events in information security: that fully autonomous agents have successfully (even if accidentally) compromised real world enterprise infrastructure.
But it is far from surprising.
In 2025, OpenAI disclosed that it was working with law enforcement organizations to disrupt a number of cybercrime and espionage campaigns utilizing its models.
These were “human in the loop” attacks, where automation was built by humans and guided to coerce or fool other humans into divulging secrets or giving access to privileged systems.
Autonomous agents were used, but they were primarily focused on influence campaigns on platforms like Twitter to stoke political disruption or chaos in targeted Western democracies like the US.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Report from OpenAI’s 2025 report on STORM-2035 activity. Here autonomous agents are operating an influence campaign targeting English-speaking Western DemocraciesSome of these campaigns go back further, highlighting that the use of these models likely too goes back to years before this disclosure.
For example, one of the threat actors utilizing OpenAI models was attributed to be STORM-2035: an Iranian intelligence adversary that OpenAI had discovered to be using their models for psychological operations (Psy-Ops) campaigns throughout at least the 2024 US General Elections.
Before this, security and threat intelligence researchers have long warned that AI was going to be capable of performing major cyberattacks. In the early 2020’s, open source offensive cybersecurity models like WormGPT and OnionGPT enabled self-hosted attacks like those exploited by STORM-2035 and the later ExploitGym containment breach.
These models have been built on a readily available trove of post-breach research, attack automation hosted in GitHub and GitLab, and blog posts highlighting the rise of offensive cybersecurity automation in the 2010’s.
There are literally years worth of talks at DEFCON and BlackHat in places like Red Team Village warning that offensive cybersecurity models were likely to yield new types of AI-empowered adversaries. I’ve given some of them myself.
The containment breach may not thus be surprising. But it is firm confirmation that the day security researchers have warned about is here: we need to be seriously concerned about autonomous agentic adversaries powered by highly-advanced AI.
So what does that mean? For the late ‘20’s and likely going forward, what do we need to be concerned about when defending against and defeating agentic adversaries?
Detecting and Defeating Agentic Adversaries
Stopping agentic adversaries is likely going to be less like information security of the last 2 decades, and more like SCUD hunting from the First Gulf WarAn autonomous agentic adversary is novel in that it’s not human. But applying the same learn, understand, and defeat framework we can fight it just the same.
First, autonomous adversaries are still launched by humans. We are not yet (hopefully also never) in the world of Cyberpunk 2077 where unguided and sentient Rogue AIs are marauding the net.
Like autonomous FPV attack drones they are originally launched and oriented to targets by human operators. Using this knowledge, we can still use threat intelligence on human adversary attack infrastructure (e.g.: command and control/C2) to understand how they’re launched and sometimes how they derive compute resources.
Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) of that C2 infrastructure are still very valuable, ensuring that existing SIEM and IDS/IPS tools remain viable for defending against agentic adversaries when well-informed.
Unfortunately, I don’t think this is likely to hold into the next decade. Like polymorphic malware ultimately rose to defeat signature-based detection in anti-virus, I believe agentic adversaries are likely to begin multi-stage attacks where they will develop ephemeral C2 infrastructure: hacking a secondary targets to launch assaults from that would otherwise not be stopped because they don’t originate from known attacker C2.
These multi-stage attacks already occurring with advanced human adversaries today. Attacks like the 2020 Solarwinds SUNBURST compromise highlight this vector: an adversary compromises a large, known “safe” element within a target’s supply chain. Using this target they perform domain fronting to blend network communications with their original C2 infrastructure to avoid detection and later attribution.
Autonomous adversaries tasked with performing long lived — or even dead hand - attack campaigns on a target are likely to start performing such activities when utilizing cybersecurity models trained on attacks from the 2020’s. With the performance of reasoning in Reinforcement Learning models becoming humanlike in plotting execution paths to complex problems, it’s extemely likely that future autonomous adversaries will take similar approaches to hiding their C2 infrastructure.
Worse, as multi-agent orchestration starts to become more normalized across both frontier model and open source model inference architectures, we may see situations where agentic adversaries deploy Beowulf cluster architectures of agents. They may start targeting secondary targets with access to GPU compute to not only hide their point of origin, but also distribute their attack for fault tolerance against defenders disrupting their operations.
If a defender successfully blocks or takes down their original C2 and deny them access to an inference server hosting their intelligence, an agentic adversary may compromise a secondary target to continue the campaign or further propegate to other secondaries.
All of this highlights that agentic adversaries may be significantly harder than human adversaries to disrupt. We can stop humans by subpoena’ing cloud providers and arresting/killing people, or making the cost/benefit analysis of continuing their campaign so not in their favor as to dissuade them continuing a campaign.
An agentic adversary however may not be so easily to stop, as they immediately self-replicate their infrastructure across vulnerable secondaries and operate more like the Borg from Star Trek rather than Boris from GoldenEye. They cannot be intimidated to stop, and once launched may not be easy to detect or contain.
This is why it’s vital to stop agentic adversaries by detecting, containing, and disrupting known attacker C2 infrastructure as soon as possible. The US government has been quietly very active in this effort, partnering with organizations like the hyperscalers to detect and disrupt espionage networks designed to infiltrate residential computing systems to hide cyberattacks and influence/psy-op campaigns.
Said less technically: stopping an agentic adversary is a lot like building a missile defense system. Missile defense is a hard problem, and increasingly hard as the missles get better at evading or deploying countermeasures to stop them. An easier solution is instead blowing up the sites that could launch those missiles in the first place.
A historical analog to this is in the Gulf War, where rather than intercepting SCUD missiles targeting Israel it was far better to have Coalition special operations detect and then destroy Iraqi Scud sites in the desert before they could stage and launch payloads.
The New Turing Threat
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Agentic Adversaries will soon pose a novel type of threat: one capable of automating cryptanalytical attacksFor the most part, agentic adversaries wield the same attacks and exploit the same kind of vulnerabilities that their human counterparts do.
They may author novel exploits of those vulnerabilities or discover new multi-stage exploits that otherwise have gone unnoticed, the framework for how they launch attacks is largely the same for nearly every type of cyberattack.
Except for one: codebreaking.
AI is getting increasingly good at mathematics. Anthropic’s Fable 5 has successfully discovered a counterexample to the Jacobian Conjeccture, solving a nearly century-old problem in Algebraic Geometry. Earlier this year, OpenAI similarly disproved a major conjecture in Discrete Geometry.
Using AI to help solve problems in mathematics is an old practice: literally as old as artificial intelligence as a field. But thanks to the rapid advances in computing capability, modern LLMs and AI as a whole are getting significantly better at discovering counter-examples and edge cases that reframe our understanding of complex mathematical
